era_context: The late medieval period in Europe, marked by the fragmentation of feudal authority and the rise of national monarchies. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty (established 1368) is consolidating control after the Mongol Yuan collapse. The Timurid Renaissance flourishes in Central Asia and Persia. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire is at its height, and the Inca Empire is expanding under Pachacuti's successors.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented in Western sources)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles exist but underrepresented in English-language historiography)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (minimal written record)

events.title: Henry IV of England defeats and captures Richard II at Conway Castle; Richard's deposition formalized
events.description: Henry Bolingbroke, Duke of Lancaster, returns from exile to challenge Richard II's rule. After a military campaign, Richard is forced to surrender at Conway Castle in North Wales. Richard is deposed and Henry is crowned Henry IV, establishing the House of Lancaster on the English throne. This event marks the beginning of the Wars of the Roses (indirectly) and represents a major shift in English royal succession away from direct primogeniture. Richard dies in captivity in 1400, likely murdered.
key_figures: Henry Bolingbroke (Duke of Lancaster, later Henry IV)
Thomas Mowbray (Duke of Norfolk, supporter of Henry)

sources.name: Froissart, Chronicles (compiled early 15th century, based on earlier accounts)
Traison et Mort de Richard Deux (contemporary French account)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles document this event with consistent dating. The deposition parliament occurred in September 1399.
cross_references: 1485-001 (Battle of Bosworth Field, end of Wars of the Roses)

events.title: Timur (Tamerlane) dies in Samarkand; Timurid Empire begins fragmentation
events.description: Timur, the Turco-Mongol conqueror who built a vast empire stretching from the Mediterranean to the borders of China, dies in February 1399 at Samarkand. His death marks the beginning of the rapid fragmentation of his empire among his sons and grandsons. However, the Timurid cultural and artistic legacy persists, particularly under his grandson Ulugh Beg, leading to a flourishing of Persian and Islamic art, astronomy, and literature in the 15th century. The empire's military dominance ends, but its cultural influence endures.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (compiled early 15th century, based on earlier sources and oral tradition)
Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur (written mid-15th century)

events.certainty_note: Timur's death in February 1399 is well-attested in multiple sources. The exact date (February 18 or 19) is traditional but consistent across sources.

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming court; preparation for future voyages
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, is rising in prominence at the court of the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424). Though his famous voyages begin in 1405, by 1399 he is already a trusted military and administrative figure. The Ming Dynasty is consolidating its power after the Jianwen Emperor's accession in 1398. Zheng He's position reflects the Yongle faction's growing influence, which will culminate in the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (then Prince of Yan, planning usurpation)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Records, compiled contemporaneously)
Luo Maodeng, Zheng He's biography (compiled later, based on official records)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's exact role in 1399 is not precisely documented. He is known to have been active in the Yongle faction's military campaigns (1399–1402), but specific events for 1399 are inferred from broader chronologies.

events.title: Jianwen Emperor's reign faces internal instability; Yongle faction consolidates power
events.description: The Jianwen Emperor, who ascended in 1398 at age 21, faces mounting pressure from his uncle, the Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor). The Jianwen Emperor's attempts to reduce princely power through the 'Removal of the Princes' (Jingnan) campaign provoke resistance. By 1399, the Prince of Yan is openly preparing for rebellion, which will erupt in 1402. This period represents a critical moment of dynastic instability within the Ming, though the dynasty itself remains stable.
events.certainty_note: The political tensions of 1399 are well-documented in Ming records. The Jianwen Emperor's reign (1398–1402) is thoroughly chronicled.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates control in the Balkans; Bayezid I expands territory
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), continues Ottoman expansion in the Balkans and Anatolia. By 1399, the Ottomans control much of the Balkans, including parts of Serbia, Bulgaria, and Greece. Bayezid is consolidating Ottoman power and preparing for further expansion. The Ottoman military system, based on the Janissary corps and feudal cavalry (Sipahi), is becoming increasingly formidable. This period marks the Ottoman Empire's emergence as a major regional power.

sources.name: Byzantine sources (contemporary accounts of Ottoman expansion)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in 1399 is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and contemporary Byzantine sources. Specific military campaigns for 1399 are less precisely dated than major battles.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Henry IV's deposition of Richard II is sometimes overstated as a 'revolution' in English governance; modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a factional coup within the nobility, not a fundamental break with feudal monarchy. The Wars of the Roses (1455–1487) are often attributed to Richard's deposition, but this is a post-hoc narrative; the actual causes were more complex and rooted in the weakness of Henry VI (r. 1422–1461), not Richard's deposition. Timur's death in 1399 is sometimes dated to 1405 in older sources, but modern scholarship confirms February 1399. The Timurid 'Renaissance' is sometimes romanticized as a unified cultural movement; in reality, it was fragmented and regionally variable, with significant violence and instability during the succession crisis.
historiographic_note: The year 1399 is well-documented in European sources (particularly English and Ottoman), moderately documented in Chinese sources, and less well-documented in African, Southeast Asian, and American sources. European sources are heavily biased toward political and military events; cultural and economic history are underrepresented. Chinese sources are official and court-centric, potentially obscuring local and popular history. Ottoman sources are largely compiled after the fact (15th–16th centuries) and may reflect later political narratives. The absence of documentation for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific does not mean nothing significant occurred; it reflects the geographic bias of surviving written records and modern historiography.

graph_edges.note: Henry IV's deposition of Richard II led to Richard's death in captivity in 1400.
Timur's death in 1399 weakened the Timurid Empire, indirectly contributing to Bayezid I's confidence before his defeat by Timur's successor in 1402.
The Jianwen Emperor's political instability in 1399 directly led to the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402.
Ottoman expansion under Bayezid I was occurring simultaneously with the Yongle usurpation in China, representing parallel processes of imperial consolidation.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
